header.headline: Quantization formats compared
header.subtitle: A generic board can compare byte layouts, header fields, and bug-risk patterns without baking format names into the DSL.

legend.title: Reusable comparison parts
legend.note: The scene program stays generic while the payload carries specific format examples.

left.title: Symmetric 4-bit
left.subtitle: The smallest header keeps the layout easy to read and the byte story compact.
bullets: Use when the board needs a clean baseline lane
Dequant math can stay simple and visual
The byte budget is easy to summarize in one metric card
left.note: This lane is the reference point for the rest of the board.

center.title: 4-bit with min
center.subtitle: An extra header field buys a wider representational story but increases the bookkeeping burden.
bullets: Header growth should be explicit in the board
Extra fields create new bug zones
The visual lane should make the tradeoff obvious
center.note: This lane explains why one extra field changes the entire block story.

right.title: Advanced packed blocks
right.subtitle: Sub-scales, high bits, and richer packing need more explanatory chips and stronger caution cards.
bullets: Packed layouts need clearer visual grouping
The board should surface bug zones explicitly
Arithmetic notes belong in payload, not the scene grammar
right.note: This lane proves the family can carry a denser comparison without becoming a table clone.

metric_1.label: Bits per weight
metric_1.detail: Every lane gets a compact byte-budget summary

metric_2.label: Header fields
metric_2.detail: Header growth drives the comparison story

metric_3.label: Bug risk
metric_3.detail: Packed fields need explicit caution cards

metric_4.label: Dequant rule
metric_4.detail: Arithmetic copy belongs in payload notes

callout_1.title: Teach arithmetic through payload
callout_1.body: The family can frame byte math, but the actual equations and edge cases should stay in content fields.

callout_2.title: Let the renderer own visual hierarchy
callout_2.body: The model should choose lanes, metrics, and callouts. The compiler should turn that into a strong board without case-specific hacks.

footer.note: This gold pack uses one generic comparison-board grammar across multiple comparison topics so the tokenizer can learn reusable visual vocabulary instead of asset names.
